section.title: Security Considerations
section.t: All of the security considerations to apply here. In addition, this document addresses how security mechanisms, such as Kerberos V5 GSS-API , can be applied to the loosely coupled model.

section.title: Loosely Coupled
section.t:
The combination of the metadata server and the storage devices consitute a storage cluster, or more simply, cluster. If an implementation chooses to employ RPCSEC_GSSv3 via an RPC-application-defined structured privilege assertion in a manner described in Section 4.9.1 of , then a single security realm is used by the clustered system. If however the implementation utilizes ffds_auth to transfer security credentials, then two security realms are used by the cluster:
Organizational - the RPC credentials used for a client to authenticate to the metadata server are generated by an external security server. The metadata server has no special priveleges with this server.
Cluster - the RPC credentials used for a client to authenticate to the storage device are generated by a security server internal to the cluster. The metadata server has special priveleges with this server.

Inside the cluster realm, the storage devices are considered an extension of the metadata server. This may mean that under a Kerberos V5 GSS-API based system, the metadata server has a copy of the private key for the storage device. Also, the special privelge could mean one or more of the metadata server beinbg colocated with the Kerberos Domain Controller (KDC) (AI: check), the metadata server has access to the KDC database, or the metadata server is allowed by the KDC to use S4U2Proxy. (AI: cite)

Such "credentials" should be limited to just the data file be accessed. Using Kerberos V5 GSS-API , some possible approaches would be:
a dedicated/throwaway client principal name akin to the synthetic uid/gid schemes.
authorization data in the ticket.
an out-of-band scheme between the client and metadata server.

Depending on the implementation details, fencing would then be controlled either by expiring the credential or by modifying the synthetic uid or gid on the data file. I.e., if the credentials are at a finer granularity than the synthetic ids, it might be possible to also fence just one client from the file.
